exemplar.title: Starting a Reading Blog for Young Adults

detailedExemplarData.title: Starting a Reading Blog for Young Adults

strengths: Clear identification of the âaccessing informationâ ATL skill and linkage to the studentâs research process, framing how information gathering supported the project
weaknesses: The analysis of how annotation strategies deepened literary understanding is superficial and needs concrete examples
Interpretation of StoryGraph visualizations lacks discussion of patterns in the rating distribution
Genre preference conclusions are not grounded in specific quantitative evidence (exact numbers or percentages are omitted)
The impact of Opinion Stage quizzes on user engagement is asserted without supporting interaction or feedback data
Design decisions are not validated by user feedback or analytics, leaving the effectiveness of navigation and engagement enhancements unsubstantiated
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: The student demonstrates awareness of key ATL skills but does not provide sufficient evidence of their effective application. Strengthening this section requires detailed examples, quantitative data, and user feedback to show how each skill contributed to both learning and product quality.

strengths: Effective connection of the learning goal to personal reading experiences, using detailed examples to ground the project rationale
Strong sequencing in the project timeline, demonstrating awareness of key phases and their order
weaknesses: Product description lacks an explicit statement of format and key features, reducing clarity on deliverable functionality
Success criteria for the learning goal are not measurable (for example, number of genres analyzed is undefined)
Product success criteria appear truncated or incomplete, obscuring targets for evaluation
Required resources and tools for each task are not specified, limiting demonstration of feasibility
Planning for data analysis is missing from the timeline, weakening alignment with the learning goal
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: While the plan is underpinned by a clear personal rationale and organized into logical stages, it falls short in specifying the deliverableâs format, defining measurable criteria, and detailing the resources and analysis steps needed to achieve both the product and the learning goal.

strengths: Use of peer ratings to evaluate interactive features provides an initial measure of user response
Single-point chart clearly identifies broad areas of success, highlighting strengths succinctly
Reflection on time management routines shows insight into personal process improvement
Self-evaluation of interactive features recognizes the role of user engagement in product quality
weaknesses: Peer feedback is presented numerically but lacks qualitative summaries to contextualize scores
Insights on personal growth are broad and need specific examples (e.g., journal entries or survey responses)
Discussion of open-mindedness is abstract; a concrete example of reading outside the comfort zone would strengthen the point
Claims about improved routines are not linked to evidence of milestone achievement or project advances
The single-point chart would benefit from precise metrics to contrast strengths and weaknesses across criteria
Summary insights are not explicitly connected to success criteria or future planning, reducing the utility of the reflection for next steps
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: The reflection shows genuine engagement with self-assessment and acknowledges areas of personal and product growth. To deepen this analysis, the student should integrate qualitative feedback, concrete examples of learning, and explicit links between reflection insights and the established success criteria.

annotations.comment: Expand your product description to explicitly state the deliverableâs format and key features, ensuring clarity on how the website will function and serve young readers.
Nice use of peer ratings; consider summarizing key qualitative feedback to give richer context to the numeric scores.
You effectively connect your learning goal to personal reading experiences with detailed examples, strengthening the authenticity of your project rationale.
Consider adding measurable success criteria for your learning goal, for example defining how many genres youâll test or how youâll quantify your reading preference.
Ensure each product success criterion is fully visible and clearly described; the image criterion appears truncatedâstate the complete measurable target.
Strong sequencing in your timeline; enhance it by specifying required resources or tools for each task to demonstrate feasibility.
Define success criteria for your learning goal itself (e.g., number of genres analyzed), not solely for the product, to demonstrate comprehensive planning.
Integrate clear planning for data analysis in your timelineâe.g., schedule dedicated time to evaluate reading preferencesâto align plan steps with your learning goal.
Provide analysis of how your annotation strategy specifically deepened your literary understanding, demonstrating the effectiveness of this ATL skill more explicitly.
Good identification of the âaccessing informationâ ATL skill and clear linking to your research process; this frames your learning approach effectively.
Effective use of StoryGraph visualization to support genre preference; consider discussing patterns in rating distribution to deepen your interpretation.
Ground your genre conclusions in specific evidence by referencing exact numbers or percentages from the chart to strengthen your argument.
Clarify how using Opinion Stage quizzes influenced user engagementâcite feedback or interaction data to show the ATL skillâs impact on product quality.
Show user feedback or analytics demonstrating how your design choices improved navigation or engagement to evidence the ATL skill application.
Support your insights on personal growth with specific examples, such as survey results or reflective journal entries, to strengthen your reflection.
Your discussion of time management is insightful; consider outlining how your new routines materially improved project milestones to evidence growth.
Link your reflection on open-mindedness to concrete momentsâdescribe a specific book choice that challenged your comfort zone and how you responded.
Strengthen this by referencing specific examples of how the project influenced your approach to future research tasks, connecting to real academic scenarios.
Your single-point chart clearly identifies successes; enhance its depth by adding precise metrics for contrasting areas of strength and improvement across all criteria.
Good self-evaluation of interactive features; detail how user polls informed content decisions to link experience to product adjustments.
Connect your summary insights to specific success criteria outcomes to demonstrate how product strengths and weaknesses influenced your learning and future planning.
